items.title: Emergency Pod: Claude Fable
items.textEn: Last Tuesday, Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 went live as the most powerful model in the world. By Friday it was dark, pulled offline after a private "is-informed" letter from Commerce’s Bureau of Industry and Security restricted foreign access to it, apparently triggered by a jailbreak concern and a phone call from Amazon’s Andy Jassy. An administration that spent two years making fun of the Biden admin for regulatory AI overreach showed they really just caught the AI safety bug. To make sense of it, we’re joined by Chris McGuire , longtime State Department and White House civil servant who worked AI and chips now at CFR. We discuss… The 5:21 PM letter that took the world’s most powerful model offline in a single day Why the ‘let it rip’ admin pivoted to mandatory AI regulation overnight. The overseas-subsidiary loophole, the Sunday emergency patch, and the foundry gap still wide open Listen now on your favorite podcast app . Live Tuesday, Dark by Friday Jordan Schneider: My mayor Muslim My bagel Jewish. Fable is fried. Knicks in five. Congratulations to the New York Knickerbockers and the city of New York on their first championship in 53 years. And a hearty — perhaps — message of concern for the Trump administration’s Bureau of Industry and Security at Commerce, which has been taking care of America’s lead about as well as De’Aaron Fox took care of the basketball over the past two weeks. To discuss why Trump took Fable offline, what’s going on with chip and AI loopholes, and what this all means for the future of AI development in America and beyond, we’re joined today by native New Yorker and longtime State Department and White House civil servant covering AI, chips, and whatnot. You know him, you love him — Chris McGuire. Welcome back to the show. Chris McGuire: I’ve been a Knicks fan much longer than I’ve been any of those things, so that’s the most important thing right now. But yeah — at your urging, had to wear the jersey to celebrate. Won’t be taking it off for at least a week, or until it smells. Jordan Schneider: Just a brief recap. We recorded a ModelTalk episode on Thursday of last week where we were all talking about our Fable impressions — Anthropic’s latest model. Now we have a nice little note when you go to Claude.ai saying Claude Fable 5 is currently unavailable. Why is that? Still sort of unclear, but the facts we know: at some point on Thursday, Andy Jassy gets on the phone with someone in the Trump administration and says, hey, there’s something fishy about this whole Claude model. We found some jailbreaks which can do something. There was some sort of communication between Anthropic and various corners of the Trump administration. And then at 5:21, we get a new export control — and I hand over the reins of this narration to Chris. So what did Commerce put out, and what were the implications? Chris McGuire: Yeah, so there was an is-informed letter — some of the details are still not publicly reported, but basically it was a private letter sent to Anthropic that one way or another regulated the export of the model, the remote access of the model, or the ability to access the model via an API from other countries, or from foreign nationals inside the United States. The Commerce Department has regulated the export of models before, in the Biden administration. The Trump administration actually, interestingly, is not enforcing those particular export controls, but has now introduced these. The authority to regulate foreign persons is called a deemed export control. In the Biden administration we actually generally exempted most of the emerging tech controls from deemed export controls, because of concerns over how it would impact business in the United States. But you can say that a foreign national inside the United States requires a license to receive a good that is controlled for export outside the United States. And then it’s a little unclear how all this fits together in a way that actually regulates API access. There are probably ways you could do it, but without seeing the letter, it’s a little hard to tell. The net result is that it was illegal to export the model, or use the model, by any foreigner — either outside or inside the United States — which obviously made it very hard to serve the model even in the United States, because you’d have to verify who end users were. Presumably they’re actually probably employees at Anthropic who are foreign nationals, who all of a sudden are restricted from accessing the model that they work on. So this obviously put the use of it to a stop. And now it seems like there are discussions going on today to try to resolve this. Jordan Schneider: So first off — is Andy Jassy the Jeremy Sochan of this story? Jeremy Sochan, of course, drafted by the Spurs, worked there for three years, got cut, shows up to the Knicks and wins a championship by spilling all of their internal workings and secrets. Chris McGuire: I don’t know what the motivation is. It’ll be interesting to see, on the Knicks side, how much of a behind-the-scenes role Sochan played in the championship, and how much we had all the intel due to a crafty signing mid-year. Underreported part of the series. Jordan Schneider: Or did the Knicks get access to Mythos during the Glasswing window, use it to hack Wemby’s email, get all the dirt on him in order to whisper disturbing things while he was taking free throws? All right, we’ll stop with this. Sorry. Chris McGuire: Wouldn’t be the first time a sports team hacked another one for competitive advantage. But yeah, I don’t think it happened here. Jordan Schneider: So clearly there’s an overlay of bad blood, or at least no love lost, between this administration and Anthropic. But the more remarkable piece of this story from my perspective: you had this administration talking for two years about how they wanted capitalism to run free, that AI shouldn’t be regulated, that this was important to American development and leadership, and that those safety nicks in the Biden administration were really overreacting. And then you go from that to an executive order which was going to be not all that tight — watered down even more when it came out two weeks ago — to all of a sudden, over the course of apparently 24 hours, deciding that the most powerful model in the world, which is an instantiation of America’s lead over the rest of the world, needs to no longer have access. So, Chris, I’m curious for your reflections on that arc, and maybe what it tells you about the future of AI policymaking. The Sea Change Chris McGuire: The particulars of this individual incident aside, the administration very clearly has fundamentally changed its policy on AI over the last few months. I think the Mythos release really did cause a sea change in Washington on AI policy. The policy has not been "let the private sector rip" for four months or so. I think you saw the culmination of that in the AI executive order, which put forward a voluntary safety regime — but ultimately it’s obviously kind of mandatory. I think this basically shows that it’s mandatory. So it’s logical. This is a real problem, and the administration is realizing that AI regulation, and the very powerful capabilities these models have, requires regulation. To the administration’s credit, they’ve recognized that and pivoted, at least in a broad philosophical sense. They haven’t pivoted all elements of their policy, and they’re also moving in ways that are very reactive and fairly chaotic. What’s clearly needed is a policy that’s not just reactive — where there’s a framework set out of, here’s the expectations, here’s how we regulate things domestically, here’s how we regulate them internationally, here’s what people can and can’t do. And it has a degree of durability. I say a degree, because this is regulating at the frontier and it’s hard. We did this in the Biden administration, and there were degrees to which we’d have to update the controls, and then business would yell at us and say, well, this isn’t predictable. And we’d say, we’re doing our best — we’re trying to create as predictable an environment as we can while responding to national security risks. But we did have some degree of a framework out there. The Trump administration needs to do that. They need to be clear about what is and isn’t allowable domestically. There are apparently some standards in the AI EO for how they’d do model evaluation, but they’re classified — probably helpful if those were public. But regardless, you could work with it. There aren’t that many companies. You also need a corresponding international strategy, of which I think we have none. How are you going to ensure AI infrastructure globally is safe? How are you going to make sure that other countries, when they start making this level of capability — which they will, very soon — are not used against us in the ways the administration is correctly worried that people could inadvertently use current-generation models against us too? There needs to be a comprehensive strategy. We’re just not seeing that. And I feel like that’s a trope in Washington — "we just need a strategy" is what everyone says to anything, so I almost hesitate to say it. But export controls in particular are a powerful, useful tool. A tool that can ensure technology is given to the people you want it given to, and create a kind of international nonproliferation regime to a degree. But instead we have this insane situation where right now Canadians are prevented from accessing these US models, but meanwhile the chips that make the models are free to be sent to China because of loopholes the administration created. So look, I hope the administration recognizes that AI regulation holistically is important, and that this is an indicator export controls are an important and powerful tool…
items.summaryZh: Anthropic的Claude Fable 5在发布后因美国商务部的出口管制信函被迫下线，凸显了特朗普政府对AI监管政策的快速转向以及现有出口控制在芯片与模型之间的漏洞，对投资者关注监管环境和技术供应链风险尤为重要。
items.translationZh:
标题：紧急舱：Claude Fable

正文：上周二，Anthropic的Claude Fable 5作为全球最强模型上线。到了周五，它因商务部工业与安全局（BIS）发出一封私人“已知信息”（is‑informed）信函而被下线，这封信限制了对该模型的海外访问，显然是因一次越狱（jailbreak）风险以及亚马逊CEO Andy Jassy 的电话所致。一个曾两年嘲笑拜登政府对AI监管过度的管理层，终于真正遇到了AI安全难题。为了解析此事，我们邀请了长期在国务院和白宫工作、现任CFR（美国富兰克林基金会）研究AI和芯片的Chris McGuire。我们讨论了……

- 5:21 PM 的信件在一天内让全球最强模型离线
- 为什么“一放任”政策在一夜之间转向强制AI监管
- 海外子公司漏洞、周日的紧急补丁，以及仍然巨大的代工（foundry）缺口

立即在您喜欢的播客应用收听。上周二上线，周五暗下

Jordan Schneider：我的市长是穆斯林，我的贝果是犹太人。Fable 被炸了。Knicks 赢了五场。恭喜纽约尼克斯以及纽约市53年来首次夺冠。并向特朗普政府商务部的工业与安全局发出一则——或许是——关切信息，该局对美国的领头企业管理得像De’Aaron Fox过去两周对篮球的表现一样糟糕。我们将讨论特朗普为何让Fable下线、芯片与AI漏洞的现状，以及这对美国乃至全球AI发展的意义，特邀纽约本土、长期在国务院和白宫负责AI、芯片等事务的资深公务员Chris McGuire。欢迎回到节目。 

Chris McGuire：我比任何身份都更是尼克斯粉，这是目前最重要的事。但好吧——在你的催促下，我确实穿上了球衣庆祝。至少一周内不会脱下，或者等到它闻起来为止。

Jordan Schneider：简要回顾。上周四我们录制了ModelTalk节目，讨论了对Anthropic最新模型的印象。现在Claude.ai页面显示Claude Fable 5不可用。原因仍不完全清楚，但已知事实包括：周四某时，Andy Jassy 给特朗普政府的某人打电话，提到Claude模型存在可疑之处。我们发现了一些越狱手段。Anthropic 与特朗普政府多方沟通。随后在5:21，我们收到新的出口管制——接下来把叙事交给Chris。

Chris McGuire：那是一封已知信息（is‑informed）信件——细节仍未公开，但本质上是一封私下发送给Anthropic的信，规定了模型的出口、远程访问或通过API向其他国家、或在美国境内的外国公民提供的权限。商务部此前在拜登政府时期已经对模型进行过出口管制。特朗普政府有趣的是，虽然之前并未执行这些出口管制，但现在却引入了新的规定。这类针对外国人员的监管被称为视同出口控制（deemed export control）。在拜登政府期间，我们通常对大多数新兴技术的视同出口控制予以豁免，以免冲击美国商业。但从法律上讲，外国人在美国境内若要接收受出口控制的商品，仍需许可证。因此，如何将其适用于API访问仍不明朗。没有看到信件全文，很难判断细节。结果是，任何外国人——无论在美国境外还是境内——使用或导出该模型都被视为非法，这使得即便在美国本土也难以提供该模型，因为必须核实终端用户身份。显然，这包括Anthropic的外籍员工，他们突然被禁止访问自己研发的模型，导致服务被迫停止。现在看来，相关讨论仍在进行，试图解决此事。

Jordan Schneider：那Andy Jassy是这件事的Jeremy Sochan吗？（Jeremy Sochan曾被马刺队选中，三年后被裁，转投尼克斯并凭借泄露内部机密夺冠。）

Chris McGuire：我不知道动机是什么。会很有趣看到尼克斯方面到底有多少幕后操作，以及我们是否因一次巧妙的中期签约而获得情报。 

Jordan Schneider：或者尼克斯在Glasswing窗口期间获取了Mythos，黑进Wemby的邮箱，获取他所有信息，以便在他投篮时进行干扰？好吧，我们暂停。抱歉。

Chris McGuire：球队之间的黑客攻击并非首次。但我觉得这里并未发生。

Jordan Schneider：显然，这届政府与Anthropic之间关系紧张，甚至可以说毫无好感。但对我而言，更惊人的是：该政府两年间大谈资本主义自由、AI不应受监管，称这对美国发展与领导力至关重要，并嘲讽拜登政府的安全担忧。随后在短短24小时内，从一个相对宽松的行政命令（两周前刚公布）转变为决定全球最强模型不能再被访问，这实际上是美国在全球AI领先地位的象征。Chris，我想听听你对这一政策转变的看法，以及它对未来AI政策制定意味着什么。

Chris McGuire：抛开个别事件不谈，政府在过去几个月显然彻底改变了AI政策。Mythos的发布真的在华盛顿引发了海潮（sea change）。过去四个月左右的政策不再是“让私人部门随意发挥”。我们在AI行政命令中看到的所谓自愿安全制度，实际上已经是强制性的。这表明监管是必然的。政府已经认识到了这一点，并在宏观哲学层面做出了转向。虽然他们并未在所有细节上转变，而且行动往往是被动且混乱的。显然需要一种不仅仅是被动响应的政策框架——明确期待、国内监管、国际监管以及可做与不可做的界限，并具备一定的持久性。由于这是前沿技术，制定框架本身就很难。我们在拜登政府时也尝试过，但企业会抱怨缺乏可预测性。我们尽力在保证国家安全的同时提供相对可预测的环境。但我们已经有了某种框架，特朗普政府也需要这样的框架，明确国内哪些行为可行、哪些不可行。行政命令中有一些模型评估标准，但已列为机密——如果公开可能更有帮助。公司并不多，制定相应的国际策略也同样重要，但目前我们几乎没有。如何确保全球AI基础设施安全？当其他国家很快具备同等能力时，如何防止被用于对抗我们——这些都是政府正确担忧的点。我们需要一个全面的战略。华盛顿常谈“我们需要一个战略”，我也几乎不敢轻易下结论。但出口管制是一个强有力的工具，可以确保技术只交给我们想要的对象，并在一定程度上形成国际不扩散体系。但现在的情况荒诞：加拿大人被禁止使用这些美国模型，而制造模型的芯片却因政府留下的漏洞可以自由出口到中国。希望政府认识到整体AI监管的重要性，并将出口管制视为关键且强大的手段。

items.textEn: A couple of days ago, “SouthernValue” tagged me in the below tweet: Can we get a @borrowed_ideas and @evrgn11112231 podcast or spaces to discuss $META state of affairs? New regulation, AI model delays, internal chaos, tokenmaxxing then unmaxxing, agentic business prospects, Neocloud prospects (and willingness / timing) … lots to discuss. — SouthernValue (@SouthernValue95) June 14, 2026 Since I also received a handful of emails asking about some of the topics raised by the tweet, I decided to share some brief and crisp thoughts on them behind the paywall. Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! items.title: The Geography of Time
items.textEn: Programming Note : As a reminder, every Sunday, I write pieces that are predominantly based on personal experiences which may or may not be loosely connected with investing. If you are reading MBI Deep Dives everyday, I think it would be rather useful for my readers to understand my personal lens a bit better since that presumably affects (at least in some capacity) the way I analyze businesses as well. After three and half years, I went to my home country: Bangladesh with my wife and son Nile. We were concerned how one and half year old Nile would cope with the grueling 20-hour flight from SFO to Dhaka (with six hour transit in Dubai), but thankfully it was uneventful. He was curious and tad bit excited at the first sight of Bangladesh. While I spent my first two and half decades in Bangladesh, it’s been almost a decade since I visited Bangladesh during the summer. Most people living abroad tend to visit the country during winter as the summer heat can be quite punishing. We still decided to go for it because we wanted to celebrate Eid with our family. However, right after landing, the humid, musty Bangladesh summer got us all drenched in sweat while rummaging for our luggage. I wasn’t bothered by the heat; if anything, I was almost nostalgic. The scent of hot, humid Bangladesh summer can be unpleasant to the unfamiliar but to someone who grew up there, it was a return to the familiar milieu that got lost for a decade. I was a little worried how Nile would handle the heat, but even he appeared to be nonchalant about it and was busy watching people. For the uninitiated, Bangladesh is the size of the New York state but has half the population of the US. So, if you are into peoplewatching, Bangladesh has plenty to offer! As we headed from the airport to my brother’s place, I was pleasantly surprised by the elevated expressways which likely cut down the time to our destination by at least half (if not more). I often joke with my friends that perhaps half the people who leave Bangladesh decide to do so while being stuck in traffic as they wonder there must be more to life than spending two-three hours everyday in traffic. I only learned to drive after moving to the US as we never had a car growing up in Bangladesh. But now that I know how to drive, it was quite a nerve wracking experience just sitting in the car in Bangladesh. There is traffic everywhere and hardly anyone is following any traffic rule. Frankly speaking, I felt if you are commuting in Bangladesh, you should consider everyday a little miracle that you made it back in one piece. After spending just one night in Dhaka, we traveled the next day to Bogura , the small city in Northern Bangladesh where both my wife and I grew up. Nile received quite the reception from both sets of grandparents. Thankfully, since both grandparents’ place is literally two-minute walking distance from each other, Nile was busy hopping from one grandparent to another everyday. My romanticism about Bangladesh started to fade the more time I spent in Bogura. The reality is even though I migrated to the Northern Hemisphere back in 2017, it was a long road of struggle to make it as a first generation of immigrant. But as things started to fall into places in the last couple of years, I discovered that I have become surprisingly used to certain amenities in life. It was almost impossible to maintain my daily 10k steps habit as the summer heat and dusty air reminded me that I was giving myself too much credit for being able to build a good habit. Perhaps California weather is simply nice enough to make it easier to stick to such habit. Moreover, as I was gaining weight after consuming copious amount of unhealthy Bangladeshi food every meal, I started googling about GLP-1. The worst was, of course, that my sleep schedule got completely destroyed and by the time I could get back to some normalcy, I had to hop onto my return flight to California. It also didn’t help that I probably wasn’t in the greatest frame of mind while in Bangladesh. I was in the middle of two big work related stressors after discovering the myriad tax related complexities for owning a company incorporated in Canada but living in the US. I decided to move my business from Canada to the US which forced me to migrate all the subscriptions from Stripe Canada to Stripe US. This proved to be significantly more challenging than I expected going in and I was coordinating among Ghost, Substack, Stripe, and a developer I hired for the project. I know the payment stocks are getting killed in the market, but the whole experience reminded me that the last thing I want to change again is my payments infrastructure. Anyways, I was particularly curious about how people in Bangladesh are using AI. The fact that AI is a big deal is not news to anyone I interacted in Bangladesh. Everyone is familiar with ChatGPT, but Gemini was also frequently mentioned. While almost everyone uses the free versions, there are a handful of people who mentioned they pay for the subscription. However, they don’t buy the subscription directly from ChatGPT. Apparently, there are some “Facebook Groups” which sell ChatGPT subscriptions. They will give you a specific email address and password with access to ChatGPT subscription for ~$3-5/month. Presumably, if they give the same email address to ten people, you can see these resellers are making handsome margin (ironically almost certainly far higher than OpenAI itself). Of course, the main downside for such “subscribers” is they have no privacy as their queries can be seen by ten other people who have the access to the same account. If large part of the world needs to make a trade-off between privacy and intelligence, it’s not particularly a difficult choice for many people. However, almost everyone I talked to seems to be still confined to the “chatbot” era of AI. I haven’t heard anyone mentioning “agent” once while discussing AI in Bangladesh. Speaking of Facebook, I came across another story from a friend that is less than ideal for…Meta. My friend ordered some fish via a Facebook page. The payment went through but the products were never delivered. When he dug into see why he fell for a scam, it turns out someone cloned the Facebook page he was looking for and the scammer even managed to get thousands of likes (almost similar to the original page) presumably by promoting ads (or who knows maybe via “click farms”). What surprised my friend was that he actually spoke with the seller over the phone and confirmed the order only after speaking directly to the seller. You probably guessed where it’s going…the scammer used AI to clone the voice of the original seller! You can only imagine how much money will be lost to such sophisticated scams in the age of AI. Unfortunately, AI must be a godsend to the scammers all over the world and it’s going to be a long, difficult fight among the platforms, scammers, and users. Even in the age of AI, there are some things that never seem to change. I visited my paternal grandparents village in Bogura. I still marvel thinking about the fact that my paternal grandparent was born in 1920 and died in 2000 in the same village and yet the very land beneath his feet belonged to three different nations in his lifetime. He was born at the time the British still ruled the subcontinent, then the village was part of Pakistan after the British left in 1947, and then it changed again to be part of Bangladesh when the country became independent in 1971. Whenever I want to imagine how dynamic the world around you can be, I just remind this personal anecdote to me. Anyways, this village got access to electricity only a decade ago and every time I go there, I am struck by how unevenly distributed time really is. If California represents the vanguard of 2026, walking through this village feels like stepping through a portal into a different century entirely. I captured the below with my Meta Glasses while strolling through the village: 0:00 / 0:38 1× Our summer trip to Bangladesh was sweaty, and logistically chaotic, but seeing Nile bounce between his grandparents made the expensive 20-hour flight worth it. The romanticism of my youth might have faded into the practical realities of adulthood, but the roots remain. As we settled back into our routine under the beautiful California sun, I realized time moves differently depending on where you stand and I’m grateful for the chance to have stood in both places this summer. Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! items.summaryZh: 作者分享了回孟加拉国探亲的个人经历，重点描述了当地生活环境、基础设施和AI使用现状，以及在跨国业务迁移和支付系统调试中的挑战，对投资者理解不同地区消费者行为、技术渗透和监管环境有参考价值。
items.translationZh: 编程说明：提醒一下，我每周日都会写一些主要基于个人经历的文章，这些经历可能与投资松散相关。如果你每天都在阅读 MBI 深度报告，我认为让读者更好地了解我的个人视角会很有帮助，因为这显然（至少在某种程度上）会影响我分析企业的方式。三年半后，我带着妻子和儿子尼尔（Nile）回到了我的祖国：孟加拉国。我们担心一岁半的尼尔如何应付从旧金山（SFO）飞往达卡（Dhaka）长达 20 小时的艰苦航程（在迪拜转机六小时），但幸运的是一路顺利。他对孟加拉国的第一眼感到好奇且有点兴奋。虽然我在孟加拉国度过了前二十五年，但自从十年前的那个夏天后我几乎没有再去过。大多数在海外生活的人倾向于在冬季回访，因为夏季的炎热相当难耐。我们仍决定前往，因为想和家人一起庆祝开斋节。然而，一降落，潮湿闷热的孟加拉国夏天就把我们全淋透了汗，在找行李时更是如此。我并不在意这热度；如果说有什么感受，那就是几乎怀旧。对不熟悉的人来说，炎热潮湿的孟加拉国夏天的气味可能令人不快，但对在那儿长大的人来说，这是找回十年失去的熟悉氛围。我有点担心尼尔会怎样应付热度，但他似乎毫不在意，忙着观察周围的人。给不熟悉的人说，孟加拉国的面积相当于纽约州，但人口只有美国的一半。所以，如果你喜欢观察人群，孟加拉国有很多可看！从机场前往我兄弟家的路上，我对高架快速路感到惊喜——它们可能把我们到目的地的时间缩短了一半（甚至更多）。我常跟朋友们开玩笑说，也许一半离开孟加拉国的人是因为在交通堵塞中觉得生活还有别的可能。我是搬到美国后才学会开车的，因为在孟加拉国长大时我们根本没有车。但现在会开车，在孟加拉国坐车却让人紧张。到处都是交通，几乎没人遵守交通规则。说实话，我觉得如果你在孟加拉国通勤，每天都该把自己平安到达视为小奇迹。只在达卡住了一晚，我们第二天就前往北部小城博古拉（Bogura），那里是我和妻子长大的地方。尼尔受到了两边祖父母的热烈欢迎。幸运的是，两位祖父母的住处相距仅两分钟步行路程，尼尔每天都在祖父母家间跳来跳去。随着我在博古拉逗留的时间增多，我对孟加拉国的浪漫情怀开始淡化。现实是，尽管我在 2017 年已移居北半球，但作为第一代移民的奋斗之路仍然漫长。但随着近几年事情逐步落地，我发现自己已经意外地适应了一些生活便利。夏季的炎热和尘土让我几乎不可能坚持每日一万步的习惯，这提醒我自己对养成好习惯的自信可能有些过高。也许加州的天气本身就更适合坚持此类习惯。此外，随着我每餐摄入大量不健康的孟加拉食物，我开始在谷歌上搜索 GLP‑1（一种减肥药）。更糟的是，我的睡眠时间被彻底打乱，等到稍稍恢复正常时，我已经得赶回加州的返程航班。与此同时，我在孟加拉国的情绪也并不最佳——我正处于两大工作压力之中：一是发现拥有一家在加拿大注册、居住在美国的公司在税务上极其复杂；二是决定将业务从加拿大迁至美国，这迫使我把所有订阅从 Stripe 加拿大迁至 Stripe 美国。这比我预想的要困难得多，我需要在 Ghost、Substack、Stripe 以及我雇佣的开发者之间进行协调。我知道支付类股票近期在市场上受挫，但这次经历让我更加确信，我最不想再次更换的就是支付基础设施。总之，我对孟加拉国人如何使用 AI 特别好奇。AI 是大事，这在我接触的孟加拉国人那里并不新鲜。每个人都熟悉 ChatGPT，但 Gemini 也经常被提及。虽然几乎所有人都使用免费版，但有少数人提到他们为订阅付费。不过，他们并不是直接向 ChatGPT 购买订阅。显然，有一些“Facebook 群组”会转卖 ChatGPT 订阅。他们会提供一个特定的电子邮件地址和密码，付费约 3‑5 美元/月即可使用 ChatGPT 订阅。推测如果他们把同一邮箱给十个人使用，这些转卖者的利润相当可观（讽刺的是，这几乎远高于 OpenAI 本身的利润）。当然，这类“订阅者”的主要缺点是隐私缺失，因为他们的查询会被同一账户的其他十个人看到。如果全球大部分人必须在隐私和智能之间做出权衡，这对许多人来说并不是很难的选择。然而，我几乎没有听到任何人提及“agent”（智能体）这个概念——大家仍停留在“聊天机器人”时代的 AI。说到 Facebook，我又听到一个对 Meta 不太友好的故事。我的一个朋友通过一个 Facebook 页面订购了鱼。付款成功但商品从未送达。当他追查为何受骗时，发现有人克隆了他所找的 Facebook 页面，骗子甚至通过投放广告（或使用所谓的点击农场）获得了数千个赞（几乎与原页面相似）。令我朋友惊讶的是，他实际上在电话里和卖家通话并在确认后完成了订单。你可能已经猜到结局——骗子使用 AI 克隆了原卖家的声音！可以想象，在 AI 时代，这类高度成熟的诈骗会导致巨额损失。遗憾的是，AI 对全球骗子来说是福音，而平台、骗子和用户之间的斗争将会漫长且艰难。即便在 AI 时代，有些事情似乎永远不变。我拜访了父亲的祖父母在博古拉的乡村。我仍然惊叹于，我祖父在 1920 年出生并于 2000 年在同一村子里去世，而他脚下的土地在他的一生中归属过三个不同的国家。他出生时，英属印度仍在统治；1947 年英属印度分裂后，村子成为巴基斯坦的一部分；1971 年该国独立后，变成了孟加拉国。每当我想象世界的多变性时，我都会用这个个人轶事提醒自己。顺便说一句，这个村子直到十年前才通电，我每次去那里，都深切感受到时间分配的极度不均衡。如果说加州代表了 2026 年的前沿，走进这个村子就像踏入了另一个世纪的入口。我用 Meta 眼镜记录下了以下画面：0:00 / 0:38 我们的孟加拉国夏季之旅汗流浃背、物流混乱，但看到尼尔在祖父母之间蹦跳让 20 小时的昂贵航程变得值得。青春的浪漫主义也许已经淡入成年后的现实，但根基依旧。在加州阳光下重新回归日常，我意识到时间的流逝取决于所站之处，感激这次夏天能够两地兼顾。 订阅者可以收到每日简报以及五年多的 Deep Dives，即对 65+ 家公司的完整分析和财务模型。每日简报只是我在 Deep Dives 之间的思考记录！ 订阅 当前组合 请注意，这并非我的买卖推荐，仅是披露，以便你评估因个人持仓可能产生的偏见。始终把我的写作视为个人投资日志，切勿忘记我的目标、风险承受能力和约束条件可能与你完全不同。我的当前组合如下披露：

items.title: Notes on Egypt
items.textEn: Source . When my driver picked me up to take me to the New Capital, he was certain there had been a booking mistake. "Are you sure you want to go this way? I can take you to the pyramids or Alexandria instead." There was no mistake. But driving there, past a mural of Egypt’s President Sisi every few hundred meters (sunglasses on, pointing at renderings of his new mega-city) I didn’t know what to expect. There’s only so much you can understand about the New Capital from reading about it online. Almost all the pictures are airbrushed or shot to show only the skyline without the heaps of rubble just to the side. Even the name is confusing. After years of competitions, jury deliberations, and proposed names like "Kemet," "Wedian," and "Memphis," the city is still technically just the ‘New Administrative Capital.’ ​ Cairo isn’t sinking like Jakarta, and nobody seems to know for certain why the New Capital exists. The official reason is to relieve congestion in a city whose metropolitan population is ~20 million. Yes, parts of Cairo are incredibly dense and congested, but there are also parts that are quite nice and spacious. The New Capital isn’t just a convenient annex to Cairo’s outskirts. It’s in the middle of the desert and took us an hour to get there and, according to my driver, "two hours, if there’s traffic." On the night of Eid al-Fitr, Cairo was filled with swarms of teenage boys eager to practice their English with lost-looking foreigners. So I asked them why. Subscribe now Based on my highly formal street survey, the most common response from teenagers was that "Sisi wants to show he’s got big balls." Someone told me he wanted to be like Khufu, who built the first and biggest of the Giza pyramids. A lady who worked at one of the many lingerie shops in Cairo told me, with a look of historical resignation, "Egyptians have always liked to spend our money building great big things in the middle of nowhere." A surprisingly common response was that Sisi built the capital far away and with wide boulevards to make it revolution-proof. In a dense city like Cairo, crowds can surround a government building, but in a capital of eight-lane mega-highways, you’d need a car just to stage a protest. Several people also noted that it’s a place for the rich to escape to. This is expressed most clearly in Madinaty, the gated suburb near the New Capital that, driving through it, makes you question whether you are still in Egypt or in the suburbs of Southern California. Madinaty, a suburb 45 minutes from Cairo for for Egypt’s elite. The photo doesn’t show that it’s surrounded by desert. Source . "It’s okay," said a man in a dusted suit. "China is building it for us." Entering the New Capital [Dune theme song plays] The entrance to the New Capital is a gigantic gate rising up from miles of sand in all directions. Everywhere you go in the New Capital can basically be summarized the same way: the biggest, most gaudy thing you’ve ever heard of, surrounded by sand and rubble. Entrance gate. Source . "That is the largest mosque in Africa," my driver told me, gesturing with his cigarette at a pair of Redwood-like Minarets rising out of the sand. When I asked to stop for a photo, he didn’t take the next off-ramp or even pull over to the shoulder. He simply came to a full stop in the middle of the highway while I got out and took my picture. The mosque was empty. Largest mosque in Africa. Source . We also passed: Sports City, built to anchor Egypt’s Olympic and World Cup bids, housing more than 22 facilities, including a stadium with nearly 94,000 seats, the largest in Egypt and the second largest on the continent. Sports City. Source . Knowledge City , a $950 million tech campus, the government bills as a node in a future digital economy (reminiscent of Zhongguancun in Beijing). Knowledge City. Source . Sisi’s new presidential palace , an enormous disc-shaped structure, said to be shaped in the style of the ancient Egyptian sun god. Presidential palace. Source . An endless row of government ministry offices , each in its own monstrously large and gold-laced building, some with titles so specialized it was hard to imagine the staff filling a single floor, let alone these giant structures. Green River , the capital’s park with fields of glowing green grass running through the desertous landscape, SIX times the size of Central Park. Green River concept rendering. Source . What thematically unifies these monuments is the windswept sand that coats them all, aging the white facades as if the paint has not fully dried. Rubble and half-finished construction stretch in every direction, and stray dogs sleep in the middle of the highways. My movie comp: Bladerunner 2049 . This is no more true than in the downtown, with its cluster of towers, including the tallest building in Africa, built by the Chinese. Tallest building in Africa. China The city was originally supposed to be built by Capital City Partners, a private real estate firm led by an Emirati businessman, but that arrangement collapsed in 2015. Egypt then signed a new agreement with China State Construction Engineering Corporation (CSCEC) to build and finance the administrative core, though CSCEC ultimately narrowed its involvement to the central business district. Europeans are involved too. Siemens built much of the power infrastructure, while the monorail is being built by Alstom (UK), though China is the biggest player. Share The New Capital is being marketed as a "smart city," which in practice means a city pre-wired for surveillance infrastructure, data integration, and perhaps autonomous vehicles. I’ve written before about how China’s approach to autonomous vehicles , at home and abroad, is about more than just exporting cars but about exporting an entire infrastructure stack, reshaping the built environment so that the technology fits cleanly into it rather than the other way around. I didn’t spot any Chinese AVs testing in the New Capital (I didn’t see many vehicles generally), but the roads themselves still showed the foundations. Compared to Cairo’s honking chaos, they were wide, clearly marked, and fitted with cameras and sensors at the finished intersections. A peeling welcome sign in downtown. This is increasingly the playbook. Indonesia is building Nusantara on Borneo to replace Jakarta, with the same decongest-the-old-city rationale, heavy Chinese involvement in its smart city and surveillance infrastructure, and the same ghost-town-in-progress atmosphere, now compounded by a new president who has quietly cut the budget in half and moved on. Senegal built Diamniadio , thirty kilometers outside Dakar, with heavy Chinese financing. Malaysia’s Forest City , developed by Chinese firm Country Garden on reclaimed land near Singapore, is now empty after Malaysian political backlash and capital controls killed demand. (It is reported that Forest City was not targeted at local Malaysians but at upper-class Chinese buyers looking to park wealth abroad, for whom the seafront properties were relatively affordable compared to coastal cities like Shanghai.) China, of course, is doing this on its home turf. Xiong’an , announced in 2017 as Xi Jinping’s personal urban legacy project sits 100 kilometers southwest of Beijing, planned to absorb the capital’s non-essential functions and eventually house 5 million people. Seven years on, it doesn’t seem to be going great. To be fair, there are versions of these neo-cities that work out. Astana replaced Almaty as Kazakhstan’s capital in 1997 and is more or less functional, which is presumably why the Egyptian government sought advice from its planners. But the big question is who bears the cost if it doesn’t go well. For Egypt and countries like it, the answer is obvious. For China, it’s more complicated than it might seem. Subscribe now Chinese SOE construction firms get paid to build, not to populate. Their revenue is front-loaded, and whether the city ever fills up is largely the host country’s problem. The Belt and Road Initiative, the Digital Silk Road, all the framework agreements and memoranda of understanding make the most sense not as instruments of grand strategic maneuvering but as the answer to a simpler question: What do you do with the most formidable construction apparatus in human history once you’ve run out of things to build at home ? The risk to China comes mainly when host countries can’t service their debt (which does happen, as Sri Lanka demonstrated , when it handed over a strategically located port to a Chinese state firm on a 99-year lease after defaulting on its loans) but even then, China has often been able to extract concessions, renegotiate on favorable terms, or simply absorb the loss as the cost of keeping its construction sector employed and its political relationships warm. In Egypt’s case, the dynamic is already visible. In 2023, China converted over $9.4 billion in Egyptian debt into developmental projects and investments, acquiring some state assets in the process. If this is truly the Chinese century, build-outs like this are its texture. The grand geoeconomic questions are worth asking, but driving past millions of empty residential units, I found myself returning to a simpler one: will these cities will actually be good for the people who end up living in them? Abundance in the Desert Millions of units already built, sitting completely empty. Imagine snapping your fingers and emptying Manhattan. Source . We spent an hour trying to reach the residential districts. Every street eventually gave way to rubble about a kilometer short of the entrance, with highway signs pointing toward destinations that went nowhere, like this: What I could see from a distance wasn’t horrible. The buildings weren’t crammed together but weren’t too sprawling either (something close to what urban planners might call gentle density ), and there were hints of genuine architectural thought. Cornices, setbacks, a sens…
items.summaryZh: 文章记录了作者实地考察埃及新首都的所见所感，指出该项目规模宏大、设施齐全却多数空置，背后是中国建筑公司深度参与的“智慧城”模式，对投资者而言揭示了海外大型基建项目的政治风险和回报不确定性。
items.translationZh:
标题：对埃及的笔记

正文：来源。当我的司机来接我去新首都时，他确信预订出错了。"你确定要走这条路吗？我可以带你去金字塔或亚历山大。"并没有错误。但在开车过去的路上，穿过每隔几百米就能看到一幅埃及总统西西的壁画（戴着墨镜，指着他新宏伟城市的渲染图），我不知道该期待什么。光靠在网上阅读，你只能对新首都有这么多了解。几乎所有的图片都经过空气刷或只拍摄天际线，旁边的废墟则被刻意遮掩。连名字都令人困惑。经过多年竞争、评审和提议的名称如“Kemet”“Wedian”和“Memphis”，这座城市在技术上仍仅称为‘新行政首都’。开罗并不像雅加达那样下沉，且似乎没有人确切知道新首都为何而建。官方理由是缓解一个约2000万人口的大都市的拥堵。是的，开罗的部分地区确实密集拥挤，但也有相当宽敞宜人的区域。新首都并非是开罗郊区的便利附属，它坐落在沙漠中，驱车前往用了一个小时，司机说“如果有交通的话，两小时”。在开斋节之夜，开罗充斥着渴望与看起来迷茫的外国人练习英语的少年。我于是问他们原因。根据我极其正式的街头调查，青少年的最常见回答是“西西想展示他的胆量”。有人说他想像建造吉萨大金字塔的胡夫一样。开罗一家内衣店的女士带着历史性的无奈说：“埃及人总喜欢把钱花在偏远地区建造宏伟的大项目上”。一个颇为常见的说法是，西西把首都建得远离市中心、道路宽阔，是为了防止革命。在像开罗这样密集的城市，群众可以围堵政府大楼，但在拥有八车道高速公路的首都，甚至要组织一次抗议也需要一辆车。还有人指出，这里是富人逃离的地方。这在距新首都45分钟车程的封闭式郊区Madinaty表现得最为明显，开车穿过时让人怀疑自己是否仍在埃及，还是已进入南加州的郊区。Madinaty，这座距离开罗45分钟车程的郊区，为埃及精英服务。照片未显示它被沙漠包围。来源。"没事，"一位穿尘土套装的男子说。"中国在为我们建造。"

进入新首都 [《沙丘》主题曲响起]
新首都的入口是一座从四周沙丘中耸立的巨型大门。无论你在新首都走到哪里，都可以用同一句话概括：你见过的最大、最炫目的建筑，四周环绕着沙子和废墟。

入口大门。来源。"那是非洲最大的清真寺，"我的司机指着一对如红杉般的尖塔对我说，抽着烟。当我要求停车拍照时，他既没有驶出下一条匝道，也没有靠边停下，只是在高速公路中间把车停住，我下车拍照。清真寺内空无一人。非洲最大的清真寺。来源。我们还经过：体育城，旨在支撑埃及的奥运和世界杯申办，拥有22个以上设施，包括一个可容纳近94,000座位的体育场，是埃及最大的体育场，也是非洲第二大的。

体育城。来源。知识城，一个价值9.5亿美元的科技园区，政府宣称它是未来数字经济的节点（让人联想到北京的中关村）。知识城。来源。西西的新总统宫，一座巨大的圆盘形建筑，据称其造型源自古埃及的太阳神。总统宫。来源。无尽排的政府部委办公楼，每座楼都是巨大的、镀金的建筑，有些名称如此专业化，以至于很难想象单层能容纳多少员工，更别提这些庞大结构了。

Green River，首都的公园，绿草如茵的草坪穿过沙漠景观，面积是中央公园的六倍。Green River概念图。将这些纪念性建筑统一的主题是覆盖它们的风沙，使白色外立面显得像是油漆还未干透。废墟和未完工的建筑在四处蔓延，流浪狗在高速路中间睡觉。我的电影对比：<银翼杀手2049>。这与市中心的情形毫无二致，那里有一群摩天大楼，包括非洲最高的大楼，由中国建造。非洲最高大楼。中国

该城市最初计划由Capital City Partners（一家由阿联酋商人领衔的私营房地产公司）建设，但该合作在2015年破裂。随后埃及与中国建筑工程总公司（CSCEC）签署新协议，负责行政核心的建设与融资，尽管CSCEC最终将参与范围收窄至中央商务区。欧洲公司也参与其中。西门子负责大部分电力基础设施，单轨列车则由法国阿尔斯通（英国）建造，尽管中国仍是最大参与者。

新首都被营销为“智慧城市”，实际上意味着这座城市已预埋监控设施、数据整合平台，甚至可能配备自动驾驶汽车。我之前写过，中国在国内外推广自动驾驶汽车，实际上是要输出整个基础设施体系，重塑建筑环境，使技术能够顺利嵌入，而非让技术去适应环境。我在新首都没有看到中国的自动驾驶汽车在测试（也很少看到车辆），但道路本身已经显露出基础设施。相较于开罗的喧闹嘈杂，这里的道路宽阔、标线清晰，并在已完工的交叉口装有摄像头和传感器。市中心的一块剥落的欢迎标语。 这正日益成为一种剧本。印度尼西亚正于婆罗洲建设Nusantara，以取代雅加达，理由同样是“去拥堵”，中国在其智慧城市和监控基础设施上投入重金，且同样呈现出半废弃的进行中城镇景象，如今新总统悄悄将预算削减一半并继续推进。塞内加尔在达喀尔以外30公里的地方建设Diamniadio，也大量依赖中国融资。马来西亚的Forest City由中国公司碧桂园在新加坡附近的填海土地上开发，如今因马来西亚政治反弹和资本管制导致需求崩溃而空置。（据称Forest City并非针对本地马来西亚人，而是针对希望在海外置业的上层中国买家，因为相较上海等海岸城市，这里的海滨房产更为负担得起。）中国当然也在国内进行类似项目。雄安新区于2017年宣布，是习近平个人的城市遗产项目，位于北京西南约100公里处，计划接纳首都的非核心功能，最终容纳500万人口。七年过去，进展并不乐观。公平地说，也有一些新城项目运作良好。哈萨克斯坦在1997年将阿拉木图迁至阿斯塔纳（现努尔苏丹），基本上运作正常，这可能也是埃及政府向其请教规划师的原因。但最大的问题是，如果项目失败，谁来承担费用。对埃及及类似国家来说，答案显而易见。对中国而言，则更为复杂。国有企业（SOE）建筑公司获得的报酬是用于建造，而非用于居住。它们的收入在前期就已实现，而城市是否真正被填满则基本上是东道国的责任。‘一带一路’倡议、数字丝绸之路以及所有框架协议和谅解备忘录，最合逻辑的解释并不是作为宏大地缘政治工具，而是：当中国在本土的建筑需求几乎耗尽后，如何处理人类历史上最庞大的施工装备。对中国而言，主要风险在于东道国无法偿还债务（这种情况确实会发生，正如斯里兰卡在违约后将战略港口以99年租约交予中国国有企业的案例），但即便如此，中国往往能够争取让步、重新协商有利条款，或直接把损失吸收，作为保持建筑行业就业和政治关系温度的代价。以埃及为例，这一动态已经可见。2023年，中国将超过94亿美元的埃及债务转换为发展项目和投资，并在过程中获取了一些国有资产。如果这是真正的‘中国世纪’，这种大规模建造就是其质感。宏观地缘经济问题值得探讨，但在我穿过数百万套空置住宅单元时，我回到了一个更简单的问题：这些城市真的会让最终居住在其中的人们受益吗？

沙漠中的丰盈 已建成的数百万套单位，却完全空置。想象一下，只需打个响指，就能让曼哈顿空荡荡。来源。我们花了一小时想进入住宅区。每条街道最终都在入口约一公里处变成废墟，路标指向的目的地根本不存在，如此情形：从远处看到的并不糟糕。建筑并未堆叠在一起，也没有过度蔓延（大致可以称作‘温和密度’，这是城市规划师的说法），且有真正的建筑思考痕迹。檐口、退让、细节……

items.author: https://www.mbi-deepdives.com/feed
items.title: Adobe's Continued Deceleration
items.textEn: If you’re on X (formerly known as “twitter”), you may be under the impression that Adobe’s growth has accelerated in FY 2Q’26 as I noticed quite a few posts mentioning how total ARR has finally accelerated last quarter. Unfortunately, Adobe’s ARR included $480 million from the recent Semrush acquisition. Once you adjust for the acquisition, the organic ARR growth was 10.5% which implies a ten consecutive quarters of deceleration in revenue growth. That doesn’t quite inspire a lot of confidence! Source: Adobe What also doesn’t inspire a lot of confidence is when you notice opex growth of ~17% surpassing revenue growth of ~13% last quarter. However, this opex growth was affected by $70 million non-cash goodwill impairment on the publishing & advertising unit, $30 million loss contingency tied to a litigation settlement, and $5 million Semrush acquisition related expenses. Once you adjust for all these, opex growth would be ~14% YoY which is still a touch above revenue growth last quarter. Source: Company Filings, MBI Deep Dives, Daloopa ; yellow colored cells adjust for Adobe’s penalty paid for canceling Figma acquisition Perhaps the more pressing concern was that management maintained its FY’26 Total ARR growth target of 10.2%, but only by folding in the ~$480 million of ARR that came with Semrush which closed in April. Remember, Adobe originally guided for 10.2% ARR growth for FY’26 in their FY 4Q’25 call. So, the fact that they didn’t change the guide despite Semrush acquisition implies they have effectively guided ARR growth down in 2Q’26 call. Adjusting for Semrush acquisition, now their FY’26 growth came to only 8.3% which is almost ~200 bps below the original guide. The high single digit organic growth outcome I called “quite conceivable” in my last Adobe update is now embedded in the guide itself. Management has tried to make the case that they’re intentionally making a short-term trade-off to go after a more compelling longer term opportunity. From the call (emphasis mine): Our FY '26 total Adobe ARR growth target of 10.2% now reflects both the addition of the Semrush book of business as well as the strategic choice to accelerate MAU freemium growth and defer previously planned Creative Cloud line optimizations . We believe this is the right long-term strategy to expand our customer base and strengthen the foundation for durable growth. …We believe now is the time to aggressively acquire the next generation of Adobe loyalists. The strategic shift to acquire more freemium customers through Adobe and Firefly lowers our second half ARR growth expectations from individual subscribers. We believe these changes do make Adobe even stronger. Unfortunately, the messengers are leaving . Narayen announced his retirement in March and remains CEO only until the board finds a successor. Adobe also disclosed that CFO Dan Durn is departing. Management explicitly said the next CEO will own FY’27 planning, and Narayen said the payback from this pivot “will play out, I think, over 2027.” In other words, the team making this promise will largely not be the team delivering on it. Charitably, an outgoing CEO has nothing left to prove and is clearing the deck so his successor starts from a cleaner base. We will have to wait and see if that is indeed the case. Adobe stock has, of course, been continually punished over the last couple of years and now trades at HSD LTM EV/EBIT multiple. Source: KoyFin (MBI Deep Dives readers get 20% discount; just click here ) Many investors understandably wonder whether much of the risk is priced in, perhaps more so than deserved. Software investors who are typically not accustomed to looking at HSD multiples on actual (not fake) operating earnings can be prone to thinking that the stock is priced for death in not-so-distant future. If you feel too tempted, I encourage you to re-read my piece “ How elephants may die ”: Serious investors may scoff at the idea of narrative driving the valuation for many companies, but the reality is when it becomes very, very hard to articulate why a company will become more relevant over time and be able to protect its profit pool, the spreadsheet may not be able to rescue such a company. If anything, the spreadsheet may be a lethal tool to show that the company can grow topline for years and still fail to be attractive for the long-term shareholders. Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! Subscribe Current Portfolio Please note that these are NOT my recommendation to buy/sell these securities, but just disclosure from my end so that you can assess potential biases that I may have because of my own personal portfolio holdings. Always consider my write-up my personal investing journal and never forget my objectives, risk tolerance, and constraints may have no resemblance to yours. My current portfolio is disclosed below:
items.summaryZh: 文章指出，Adobe 在2026财年第二季度的ARR增长被并购Semrush的480亿美元并表后掩盖，剔除并购后有机ARR仅增长10.5%，已连续十个季度放缓；运营费用增速仍高于收入增速，管理层虽承诺长期增长但高层离任增添不确定性，这对投资者评估公司增长前景与估值风险至关重要。
items.translationZh:
如果你在 X（前称“twitter”）上，可能会误以为 Adobe 在2026财年第二季度的增长出现加速，因为我注意到相当多的帖子提到总 ARR（年度经常性收入）终于在上个季度加速了。遗憾的是，Adobe 的 ARR 包含了最近对 Semrush 收购带来的 4.8 亿美元。剔除收购因素后，有机 ARR 增长为 10.5%，这意味着收入增长已经连续十个季度放缓。这个数字并不能带来太多信心！来源：Adobe

同样让人担忧的是，运营费用（opex）增长约 17%，超过了上个季度约 13% 的收入增长。然而，这一运营费用增长受到 7000 万美元的非现金商誉减值（针对出版和广告部门）、3000 万美元的诉讼和解相关损失准备金以及 500 万美元的 Semrush 收购费用的影响。剔除这些因素后，运营费用的同比增长约为 14%，仍略高于上个季度的收入增长。来源：公司文件、MBI 深度调查、Daloopa；黄色单元格调整了 Adobe 因取消 Figma 收购而支付的罚金。

更紧迫的担忧是，管理层仍保持 2026 财年总 ARR 增长目标为 10.2%，但该目标已将约 4.8 亿美元的 ARR（来自 4 月完成的 Semrush 收购）计入其中。请记住，Adobe 最初在 2025 财年第四季度电话会议上指引的 2026 财年 ARR 增长目标也是 10.2%。因此，他们在第二季度电话会议中未调整指引，实际上是将有机 ARR 增长目标下调。剔除 Semrush 收购后，2026 财年的实际增长只有 8.3%，比原指引低约 200 个基点。我的上一次 Adobe 更新中提到的高单位数有机增长“相当可以想象”，现在已经嵌入了指引本身。管理层试图说明他们在短期内作出权衡，以追求更具吸引力的长期机会。正如电话会议中所说（强调为我所加）：“我们2026 财年的总 Adobe ARR 增长目标 10.2% 现在既反映了 Semrush 业务的加入，也反映了加速 MAU（每月活跃用户）免费版增长并推迟此前计划的 Creative Cloud 线优化的战略选择。我们相信这是扩大客户基数、强化持久增长基础的正确长期策略……我们认为现在是积极获取下一代 Adobe 忠实用户的时机。通过 Adobe 和 Firefly 获取更多免费版客户的战略转变降低了下半年来自个人订阅者的 ARR 增长预期。我们相信这些改变让 Adobe 更加强大。”

不幸的是，传递信息的人离开了。Narayen 在三月宣布退休，并将仅在董事会找到继任者之前继续担任 CEO。Adobe 还披露 CFO Dan Durn 将离职。管理层明确表示，下一任 CEO 将负责 2027 财年规划，Narayen 说此转型的回报“将会在2027年显现”。换句话说，作出这些承诺的团队很可能不是最终执行这些承诺的团队。宽容地说，离任的 CEO 已无所证明，正在为继任者清理舞台，使其从更干净的基础开始。我们只能拭目以待这是否会成为现实。Adobe 的股价过去几年持续受到惩罚，目前以 HSD（高净负债）最近 12 个月企业价值/息税前利润（EV/EBIT）倍数交易。来源：KoyFin（MBI 深度调查读者可享 20% 折扣；点击此处）

许多投资者自然会想，是否已经将大部分风险计入了股价，甚至可能被高估。对软件投资者而言，通常不习惯在真实（而非虚假）经营收益上使用 HSD 倍数，可能会倾向于认为该股在不久的将来已“注定死亡”。如果你过于冲动，我建议你重新阅读我的文章《大象可能会死亡》：严肃的投资者可能会嘲笑叙事驱动估值的想法，但现实是，当很难阐明一家公司为何随着时间变得更具相关性、如何保护其利润池时，电子表格可能无法拯救这家公司。事实上，电子表格可能成为致命工具，显示公司即使多年实现营收增长，仍可能对长期股东缺乏吸引力。订阅者可获得每日简报以及五年以上的深度调研，即对 65 多家公司进行完整的财务模型分析。每日简报只是我在深度调研之间的思考记录！欢迎订阅。

当前持仓
请注意，这些并非我的买卖推荐，只是为了让你评估我可能因个人持仓产生的偏见。始终将我的写作视为个人投资日记，切勿忘记我的目标、风险承受能力和约束条件可能与你截然不同。我的当前持仓如下：

items.title: 📊 PRO: This Week in Visuals
items.textEn: Welcome to the Saturday PRO edition of How They Make Money . Over 300,000 subscribers turn to us for business and investment insights. In case you missed it: 🚀 How SpaceX Makes Money 🔎 Why Google is Selling $85B of Itself Subscribe now Premium members get: 📊 Monthly reports : 200+ companies visualized. 📩 Tuesday articles : Exclusive deep dives and insights. 📚 Access to our archive : Hundreds of business breakdowns. PRO members get everything PLUS : 📩 Saturday PRO reports : Timely insights on the latest earnings. Today at a glance: ☁️ Oracle : Backlog vs. Balance Sheet 🎨 Adobe : Freemium Bet ✍️ DocuSign : IAM Crosses 13% of ARR 🎿 Vail Resorts : Worst Season On Record 🐶 Chewy : Stretched But Steady 1. ☁️ Oracle: Backlog vs. Balance Sheet Oracle beat on revenue, beat on earnings, and grew its backlog by $85 billion during the quarter to $638 billion. The stock fell 12% anyway, its worst day since December. The quarter wasn’t the problem. The incoming bill was. New CFO Hilary Maxson guided FY27 gross margins to "step down" and laid out ~$70 billion in net cash outlay, partly funded by a fresh ~$40 billion raise. Investors naturally scrutinize the cost of converting that backlog. Q4 FY26 in numbers Revenue grew 21% Y/Y to $19.2 billion ($0.1 billion beat). Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) remains the main driver: ☁️ Cloud +47% Y/Y to $9.9 billion (OCI +93% to $5.8 billion). 🌐 Software -2% Y/Y to $6.8 billion. 🖥️ Hardware +9% Y/Y to $0.9 billion. 💼 Services +13% Y/Y to $1.5 billion. Margin trends: The operating margin remained flat at 32%, while gross margin compressed by 5pp to 65% as lower-margin IaaS scaled. Cash flow: For the full year FY26, operating cash flow was $32 billion (+54% Y/Y), but that’s not enough to fund the CapEx ramp. Free cash flow stayed deeply negative (-$24 billion) as CapEx hit $56 billion, above the $50 billion plan. Balance sheet: Oracle raised $43 billion in debt and $5 billion in equity in FY26 and expects another ~$40 billion in debt and equity financing in FY27. Oracle now has a net debt of $124 billion , including operating lease liabilities. Source: Fiscal.ai FY27 guidance: Management reaffirmed FY27 revenue guidance of ~$90 billion, implying ~34% growth. Capital spending is expected to reach roughly $70 billion of Oracle’s own cash outlay. Oracle expects to raise about $40 billion through debt and equity, with any new debt likely pushed to calendar 2027. So what to make of all this? 📈 The beat the market ignored: RPO jumped 363% Y/Y and 15% Q/Q to $638 billion, well above the ~$590 billion analysts expected. But the market has stopped paying for backlog alone and started pricing the cost of delivering it. ⚖️ Less funding pressure, still massive funding needs: Roughly $75 billion of Oracle’s large AI contracts is prepaid or customer-supplied hardware. That helps. But FY27 capital outlays are still enormous, and Oracle still needs external financing. The funding story improved at the margin. The absolute numbers got bigger. 📉 Margins are the new fault line: Oracle is building capacity before it fully bills. That creates a timing gap: CapEx now, revenue later. Management says infrastructure margins recover quickly once utilization ramps, but investors want proof. 🧱 The old Oracle is now shrinking: Software revenue fell 2% Y/Y. Oracle still needs that legacy cash engine to help fund the AI infrastructure buildout, and a contracting base makes the math harder. Takeaway: Oracle delivered 1.2 gigawatts of incremental data center capacity in FY26 and expects Q1 FY27 delivery to approach 1 gigawatt. The bull case is becoming more concrete, but so are the capital intensity, margin pressure, and financing needs. Oracle is now a bet on execution. 2. 🎨 Adobe: Freemium Bet Read more
items.summaryZh: 本文概述了本周重点企业的财报与业务进展，重点分析了 Oracle 巨额订单积压与资本支出压力、Adobe 的免费增值策略、DocuSign 的收入占比突破、Vail Resorts 的季节性低迷以及 Chewy 的稳健表现，提醒投资者关注这些公司的现金流、利润率和融资需求。
items.translationZh:
标题：📊 PRO：本周可视化报告

正文：欢迎阅读《他们如何赚钱》周六 PRO 版。超过 30 万订阅者依赖我们的商业和投资洞察。如您错过：🚀 SpaceX 如何赚钱 🔎 谷歌为何在出售价值 850 亿美元的自身 立即订阅 会员可获得：📈 每月报告：200 多家公司可视化。📚 周二文章：独家深度分析和洞察。📂 访问我们的档案库：数百篇商业拆解。PRO 会员获得全部内容外加：📚 周六 PRO 报告：最新财报的及时洞察。今天一览：☀️ Oracle：积压订单 vs. 资产负债表 🎬 Adobe：免费增值赌注 ✍️ DocuSign：IAM 占 ARR 的 13% 🎖️ Vail Resorts：创纪录最差季节 🐶 Chewy：压力增大但保持稳健

1. ☀️ Oracle：积压订单 vs. 资产负债表
Oracle 在本季度收入超预期、盈利超预期，并将积压订单增长至 8,380 亿美元，达到 6,380 亿美元。然而股价仍下跌 12%，创下自去年十二月以来最差单日表现。问题不在于本季度，而在于即将到来的账单。新任 CFO Hilary Maxson 指导 FY27 毛利率将“下调”，并规划约 700 亿美元的净现金支出，其中约 400 亿美元来自新一轮融资。投资者自然会审视将这些积压订单转化为收入的成本。

Q4 FY26 关键数据
- 收入同比增长 21%至 192 亿美元（超出 10 亿美元预期）。
- Oracle 云基础设施（OCI）仍是主要驱动力：☀️ 云业务同比增长 47%至 99 亿美元（OCI 同比增长 93%至 58 亿美元）。
- 软件业务同比下降 2%至 68 亿美元。
- 硬件业务同比增长 9%至 9 亿美元。
- 服务业务同比增长 13%至 15 亿美元。
- 利润率趋势：运营利润率持平在 32%，毛利率因低利润率 IaaS（基础设施即服务）规模化压缩 5 个百分点至 65%。
- 现金流：全年运营现金流为 320 亿美元（同比增长 54%），但不足以支撑资本支出增长。自由现金流深度为负（-240 亿美元），因资本支出达 560 亿美元，超出 500 亿美元计划。
- 资产负债表：Oracle 在 FY26 融资 430 亿美元债务和 50 亿美元股权，预计 FY27 再融资约 400 亿美元债务与股权。净负债达 1,240 亿美元，已包括经营租赁负债。

来源：Fiscal.ai

FY27 指引：管理层重申 FY27 收入指引约 900 亿美元，暗示约 34% 增长。资本支出预计约为 700 亿美元自有现金支出。Oracle 预计将通过约 400 亿美元的债务和股权融资筹集资金，任何新增债务可能推迟至 2027 年日历。

那么我们该如何解读？
- 📈 市场忽视的亮点：RPO（已签订订单收入）同比增长 363%，环比增长 15%至 6380 亿美元，远高于分析师预期的约 5900 亿美元。但市场已不再只为积压订单买单，开始计价交付成本。
- ⚖️ 融资压力略有缓解，但需求仍巨大：约 750 亿美元的 AI 大合同已预付或由客户提供硬件，这有助于缓解压力。但 FY27 资本支出仍然庞大，Oracle 仍需外部融资。融资情况改善幅度有限，绝对规模在扩大。
- 📊 利润率成为新裂点：Oracle 正在先行建设容量，再进行完整计费，这导致资本支出在前，收入在后。管理层称基础设施利润率在利用率提升后会快速恢复，但投资者仍在等待实证。
- 🏛️ 传统 Oracle 正在缩小：软件收入同比下降 2%。Oracle 仍需依赖这部分传统现金引擎为 AI 基础设施建设提供资金，而合同基数的收缩让盈利模型更具挑战。
- 关键结论：Oracle 在 FY26 交付了 1.2GW 额外数据中心容量，预计 FY27 Q1 将交付约 1GW。多头案例正在变得更为具体，但资本密集度、利润率压力和融资需求同样在显现。Oracle 现在是对执行力的押注。

2. 🎬 Adobe：免费增值赌注
（后续内容略）

items.author: https://www.generalist.com/feed
items.title: Introducing Generalist Intelligence
items.textEn: Friends, For several years, I’ve wanted to launch a weekly intelligence briefing. While there are exceptional private intelligence groups covering the financial markets and geopolitics, and many great tech publications, I nevertheless found myself wanting something a little different from what I was able to find. Something that delivered an elegant, insightful analysis of the hidden shifts and fresh opportunities bubbling up in our industry. Something high-signal, low-fluff, and with, perhaps, a dash of wit. Something that provides a little greater situational awareness in thirty minutes or less. But doing it right would require taste and craft, as well as real time. For an intelligence product to succeed, it must find fresh angles on known stories and the non-obvious ones just bubbling beneath the surface. That takes scrounging and scouring. Earlier this year, we were able to unlock both. Most important was convincing one of my favorite writers, an old hand at this kind of work, to jump aboard and lead the charge. By leveraging frontier models, we’ve been able to build an extensive signal-gathering apparatus that would not have been possible to conduct without a very large team, spanning news, industry outlets, social media, academic research, government and regulatory filings, code repositories, model-adoption aggregators, fundraising data, prediction markets, website monitoring, open-source intelligence sources, and talent flows. We also monitor a select group we refer to as "super signalers" across various verticals, selected by us, who we believe are frequently ahead of the curve. Not all of these approaches are bearing fruit yet, but we expect them to improve as we build and fine-tune. Already, we’ve seen the benefits of a process that searches so expansively. Not only does it surface tidbits a human might have missed, but it spots echoes or juxtapositions that are revealed only with capacious context. However, the story selection and writing are done by us, the humans. The system hunts ceaselessly, bringing up a furious exhaust of data, noise, and signal, all in one. It is up to the human to think, judge, analyze, and write. Generalist Intelligence is the result of this marriage, a weekly intelligence briefing designed for tech’s most discerning professionals. It will arrive Friday mornings. Our hope is that it opens you up to perspectives you hadn’t considered and offers a look at fresh pockets of the future you might have missed. I’ve been reading internal versions of this as we tuned the format over the past couple of months, and have found myself looking forward to it every week. Subscribe now We’re in the very early stages of getting this right, and there’s a long way to go. We’d love to know what you think, especially what you’d like to see more of, as it comes up. Our goal is for this to become the highest signal-to-noise newsletter you receive each week, and one worth setting aside the time for. Now, onto the briefing. — Mario With SpaceX blasting into the public markets and Anthropic and OpenAI straightening their ties to follow suit, the bajillion-dollar question is, obviously: are they worth it? Anyone claiming to know the answer very likely has a bridge to sell you. Nevertheless, we can’t stop thinking about it. SpaceX feels like a special case. Public markets are being asked to price in total interstellar domination at IPO , with a good trillion of the $1.77 trillion price tag – at which the loss-making company would trade at over 90x last year’s revenue – based on a series of highly speculative goals including repeat business on Mars, data centers in orbit, and making a key contribution to the development of AI. On the other hand, it’s Elon. So, you know. What’s really got our chins wagging at Generalist Towers this week is a more existential question. Anthropic and OpenAI haven’t filed public prospectuses, but they look likely to be priced as though they are in, effectively, a two-horse race for control of the future (ok, maybe Google too). Some of us think that’s right – that it’s only a matter of time before one of the model labs cracks recursive self-improvement, and then it’s a short hop to AGI and either Sam or Dario as World King. But a couple of things have landed in the past week that have got us thinking seriously about the other side. So we thought it would be fun to take the contrarian stance, and make the bear case for AI. AI, huh, what is it good for? LLMs are incredibly good at coding. At the end of last week, Anthropic dropped an essay titled When AI builds itself , in which the Claude-maker claimed that more than 80% of the code it merges into its codebase was written by Claude, and that its engineers were shipping "8x as much code per quarter" compared to their relatively flat productivity between 2021 and 2025. (When I asked Claude about this, it raised an eyebrow at the figures, pointing out they were "self-reported by a company currently raising money on exactly this story.") And to be fair, rather gallantly, the good folks at Anthropic point out that the 8x figure is likely an overstatement as it measures "quantity over quality." But the direction is clear: Claude is incredible at writing code. Presumably, this is translating into giant gains for all? Well, no. A new study of more than 100,000 GitHub developers found that Claude Code allowed coders to create or edit almost 300% more files. But that uplift was halved to 150% by the time they got to submitting pieces of work for review, and that in turn shrunk a further 5x by the time it got to shipping. In other words, coders using the latest agentic AI tools released just 30% more software, compared to those raw-dogging it. The researchers attribute that remaining 30% to what they call "strong complementarities" between the agents and the coders, meaning the agent very much needs the coder. They also noted that while there was a marked uptick in new apps being made this way, there was "no increase" in overall demand. This doesn’t mean agents are bad at code, but it does mean they might not be nearly as good as you thought at the thing they’re best at. So what happens to the model makers? Read more
items.summaryZh: 作者宣布推出《Generalist Intelligence》每周情报简报，旨在通过大模型和广泛信息来源提供高信号、低噪音的行业洞见，帮助投资者快速捕捉技术领域的潜在机会与风险。文中还探讨了AI公司如Anthropic、OpenAI以及SpaceX上市前的估值争议，提示其实际产出可能低于市场预期，对投资者评估这些公司价值具有参考意义。
items.translationZh:
标题：Introducing Generalist Intelligence

正文：朋友们， 多年来，我一直想推出一份每周情报简报。虽然已有出色的私人情报组织覆盖金融市场和地缘政治，亦有许多优秀的科技媒体，但我仍希望能找到一些与已有资源不同的东西。一个能够以优雅、深刻的分析，揭示行业中正在酝酿的隐藏变动和新兴机会的产品。既要高信号、低噪音，或许还能带点机智。用三十分钟甚至更短的时间，提供更高的情境感知。要做好这件事，需要品味、工艺以及实时性。情报产品要成功，必须在已知故事上找到新视角，并捕捉潜伏在表面之下的非显而易见的内容。这需要大量的搜集与筛选。今年早些时候，我们终于解锁了这两项能力。最关键的是说服了我最喜欢的作者之一——一位在这类工作上经验丰富的老手——加入并领衔。通过利用前沿模型，我们构建了一个庞大的信号收集系统，如果没有一个非常大的团队，这是不可能完成的，涵盖新闻、行业媒体、社交媒体、学术研究、政府与监管文件、代码库、模型采纳聚合器、融资数据、预测市场、网站监测、开源情报来源以及人才流动。我们还监控一组选定的“超级信号源”，这些信号源覆盖多个垂直领域，由我们挑选，认为它们经常走在趋势前端。并非所有方法现在都已产生成果，但我们期望随着系统的构建与微调，它们会不断改善。已经看到，广泛搜索的流程带来了好处。它不仅能发现人类可能错过的细节，还能在宽广的语境中捕捉到仅凭局部信息难以察觉的呼应或并置。然而，选题与写作仍由我们——人类完成。系统不停地猎取数据，产生大量的噪声与信号，全部混杂在一起。最终由人类思考、判断、分析并撰写。Generalist Intelligence 正是这种人机结合的结果，一份面向科技领域最挑剔专业人士的每周情报简报。它将在周五上午送达。我们希望它能为你打开未曾考虑的视角，提供你可能错过的未来新趋势的洞见。过去几个月，我一直在阅读内部版本，调校格式，已开始每周期待它的发布。立即订阅 我们仍处于早期阶段，还有很长的路要走。我们非常希望听取你的意见，尤其是你希望看到更多的内容。我们的目标是让它成为你每周收到的信噪比最高的时事通讯，值得你专门抽时间阅读。现在，让我们进入简报。——马里奥

随着 SpaceX 进入公开市场，Anthropic 和 OpenAI 正在加紧准备跟进，显而易见的数十亿美元问题是：它们到底值不值得投资？任何声称知道答案的人，很可能只是在向你兜售桥梁。不过，我们仍旧忍不住思考。SpaceX 似乎是个特例。公开市场被要求在首次公开募股时为其全部星际统治定价——在 1.77 万亿美元的估值中，约有 1 万亿美元来自对火星重复业务、轨道数据中心以及对 AI 发展的关键贡献等高度投机性的目标——这意味着这家仍在亏损的公司将以去年收入的 90 倍以上进行交易。另一方面，它是埃隆。你懂的。我们在 Generalist Towers 本周真正感兴趣的，是一个更具存在意义的问题。Anthropic 和 OpenAI 还未提交公开招股说明书，但它们看起来很可能会以仿佛已在进行的两马拉松竞争（也许还有谷歌）来定价。一些人认为这很合理——因为模型实验室最终只会出现递归自我改进的突破，然后很快跳向通用人工智能（AGI），届时 Sam 或 Dario 将成为世界之王。但过去一周出现的几件事让我们认真思考了另一面。因此，我们决定站在逆向立场，提出 AI 的空头论点。AI，到底有什么用？大型语言模型（LLM）在编码方面非常出色。上周末，Anthropic 发布了一篇题为《当 AI 自己构建自己》的文章，Claude 的制造者声称，超过 80% 的代码是由 Claude 编写并合并到其代码库的，并且其工程师的“每季代码产出是 8 倍”，相较于 2021 至 2025 年相对平稳的生产率。 （当我询问 Claude 时，它对这些数字挑眉，指出这些是“正在为上述故事融资的公司自行报告的”。）公平地说，Anthropic 也坦率地指出，8 倍的数字可能夸大，因为它衡量的是“数量而非质量”。但方向是明确的：Claude 在写代码方面非常强大。理论上，这应当为所有人带来巨大的收益？并非如此。一项对超过 10 万名 GitHub 开发者的研究发现，使用 Claude 代码的开发者能够创建或编辑的文件数量提升近 300%。但当他们提交代码审查时，这一提升被削减至 150%，进一步在实际交付时又被压缩了 5 倍。换言之，使用最新的具备代理功能的 AI 工具的开发者，最终只比纯手工编码的开发者多交付约 30% 的软件。研究人员将剩余的 30% 归因于他们所说的“强互补性”，即代理工具仍然非常依赖开发者本身。他们还指出，虽然通过这种方式创建的新应用数量有所上升，但整体需求并未出现增长。这并不意味着代理工具在编码上表现不好，但意味着它们可能并没有想象中那样在其最擅长的领域发挥巨大效用。那么，这对模型制造商意味着什么？阅读更多

items.textEn: Programming Note: This is the final part of my FICO Series. Last month, I announced that I would start publishing my Deep Dives as a series of posts rather than one single post. Having now tried that format with FICO, I’ll be reverting back to single posts for future Deep Dives. Several paying subscribers emailed me saying they prefer reading a Deep Dive in one place, and frankly, I’ve come to the same conclusion myself: unless you’re already familiar with the company, it’s hard to keep track of the thread when the analysis is spread across multiple posts. It’s simply easier to read (or listen to) the whole thing at once. Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! Subscribe FICO's Capital Allocation and Management Incentives

items.translationZh: 编程说明：这是我的 FICO 系列的最后一篇。上个月，我宣布将把我的深度研究（Deep Dives）以系列文章的形式发布，而不是一次性完整发布。现在尝试了这种格式后，我将在今后的深度研究中恢复单篇发布。几位付费订阅者发邮件说他们更喜欢一次性阅读完整的深度研究，坦率地说，我自己也得出了同样的结论：除非已经非常熟悉公司，否则分析分散在多篇文章中很难跟上思路。一次性阅读（或收听）整篇内容要容易得多。订阅者可以获得每日简报以及五年以上的深度研究，即针对 65+ 家公司的完整分析和财务模型。每日简报只是我在深度研究之间的随想！订阅 FICO 的资本配置和管理激励

items.author: https://www.chinatalk.media/feed
items.title: Sen. Slotkin: NDAA, AI guardrails, and banning China's cars
items.textEn: of Michigan, who served in OSD Policy and three terms in the House before joining the Senate Armed Services Committee, joins ChinaTalk to break down what got in, what got voted down, and why NDAA markup days are the only two days a year the Senate acts like a functioning institution. Beyond the NDAA, we also discuss… The AI Guardrails Act, the Anthropic debate, and why no one SecWar or AI company should set the rules for the kill chain, Her bill with Bernie Moreno banning Chinese connected vehicles, The Democratic playbook if the party flips a chamber in November, Data ownership, the Midwest’s data center revolt, and why a healthy democracy would be talking about AI every single day. Listen now on your favorite podcast app . NDAA Markup—The Most Fun to Be Had in the Senate Jordan Schneider: Senator Slotkin of Michigan, formerly of OSD Policy and the House of Representatives — welcome to ChinaTalk. Senator Elissa Slotkin: Thanks for having me. Jordan Schneider: Let’s start with the new NDAA we have, sort of. What are you excited and concerned about? Let’s talk in particular about ROBOCOM, the robotic and autonomous systems combatant command that the committee was excited to kick off. Senator Slotkin: Look, the two days we do the NDAA markup in the Senate are typically my best days of the year. They are behind closed doors — which is probably why people act more normal — and there’s real substantive debate on provisions. Most importantly, say a Republican colleague has a provision and I’m like, "Look, I’d be with you if you just changed X, Y, and Z." They send their staff back to change X, Y, and Z, and three hours later we revisit it, it gets a vote, and it gets in. Or two people have similar amendments on different sides of the aisle, and they go compromise over lunch and fix it. That doesn’t happen enough in this place, so I’m always happy to be in NDAA markup. Jordan Schneider: Maybe before we do the specific stuff — is there any structural change that would make more of the building work like SASC does on NDAA markup days? Senator Slotkin: The way you would do it is probably never gonna happen, because it means you kick the cameras out. That’s the only difference — the cameras are not there, so people don’t feel like they need to play to an audience. They aren’t kicking back with their political talking points; they’re actually doing substantive work. And it’s a pretty hard argument to make: "Hey America, we’re going to be less transparent in the Senate." That’s a hard thing to do. I think there’s a million ways to make the Senate more efficient. The Senate was a caterpillar and could one day be a butterfly, but is right now in the disgusting cocoon and doesn’t know what it’s going to be. I have a million ideas on how to reform this institution. But we’re effective in the NDAA because people are not playing to the bleachers. Jordan Schneider: Do people really need those clips, though? Senator Slotkin: I don’t know that people wake up in the morning and say, "I’m so excited for my five minutes in front of an Armed Services hearing today." But those hearings have been televised for over twenty years, and no one’s gonna be the chairman who says, "I’m kicking out the public," right? And I don’t think the public feels like they want less oversight and insight into what the House and Senate are doing. So it’s a tough position. The way to deal with this is a different vision of how we use these committees — how we do work, how we build coalitions around big ideas. You were just talking about the robotics command. That’s a big deal — the idea to pass something that would potentially be a new command — and unfortunately, we don’t start discussing it until we’re really in markup. Our staff are looking at it before then, for sure, but you could imagine different ways of doing these things. I’m open to all of that. But for the reasons I described, I like NDAA markup because people quite literally roll up their sleeves and do real work. Jordan Schneider: There’s a broader, weird information-asymmetry thing here. There’s so much in the NDAA, and as a principal you can only really get your head around a few provisions. How do you manage that principal-agent issue? Senator Slotkin: Like everywhere in Washington, you have staff who are smarter than you and who are immersed in all of these things — who read everything, who go through hundreds of amendments and flag the most important, the most controversial, the most consequential, the sexiest, whatever. And you prep. If you do your homework — and some of my colleagues really do their homework, some of them don’t — if you care about substance, your staff is saying to you, "Hey, we’ve read through the two thousand pages. Here are the nineteen things we think you really need to know that are gonna come up for real substantive debate, and we’re going to make a recommendation on how you vote." We discuss all that before I ever get in that room. There are giant binders full of amendments — it’s a whole process. But no one is the master of every detail. You get smart staff who can appropriately flag the big, big strategic stuff. Jordan Schneider: Great. All right, let’s come back to my first question — though I do want to do an hour on procedural reform. Maybe for next show. Senator Slotkin: You need a life. You need a life. Jordan Schneider: What else is there to live for? Well, I watched the Knicks last night — that was incredible. What pieces of the NDAA are you excited about and concerned about? Senator Slotkin: I don’t like the Knicks, but okay. When you have two thousand pages, there are things you’ve really fought for and care about, and there are things you don’t agree with. The things I was really glad got in there: the AI Guardrails Act — actually putting some guardrails on the Pentagon and its use of AI, particularly in matters of life and death. Banning Chinese cars on all military bases — I’ve been working on this for four years, and that’s for bases here in the United States but also overseas, where Chinese cars are just everywhere. We had important provisions on Selfridge, an Air Force base in Michigan, on PFOS — making sure the military cleans up after itself when it’s contaminated local communities, including in Michigan. Then there were disappointments — things voted down that left me downright shaking my head, including my own provisions banning uniformed military from collecting ballots and voting machines, breaking the chain of custody of votes. That’s illegal anyways, and we should never be spending a dime on it. That got voted down. Also: not sending the uniformed military to voting locations. I’m very concerned about the authoritarian playbook we’ve seen over and over again, including in Hungary a couple months ago. The president has said if his party doesn’t win this election, then it was rigged. I just don’t want them to precipitate a national security threat such that suddenly they have to send uniformed military to the polls for the first time in our history. Never done that. And we genuinely didn’t talk about the war in Iran. It was this massive elephant in the room. We know they’re all talking about $350 billion, $400 billion as the cost of the war right now — and it’s not over. Could we please discuss that? This war isn’t authorized, so are we gonna appropriate money for a war that’s not authorized by Congress? The top line is huge, and they’ve slashed spending on domestic issues. So there were big strategic issues at the big-picture level, and then a lot of things I was glad got in. AI and the Kill Chain Jordan Schneider: Let’s pick up on a few of those themes. AI starting a nuclear war is something where it’s pretty easy to see the consensus. But as you think down from the strategic to the operational and tactical level, there are a lot of really exciting applications. I was just thinking — to what extent could Claude Mythos do your old job at OSD Policy? Would I rather have a chatbot negotiating an Iran peace deal than Witkoff? Maybe! Probably! What makes sense, and what should people be concerned about? Because clearly there’s going to be a real edge from an effectiveness perspective if you can figure out how to use these tools right. Senator Slotkin: There’s already experimentation going on across our economy on AI, and certainly at the Pentagon — there has been for years. What the whole debate with Anthropic brought up is that there shouldn’t be any one Secretary of Defense or any one company deciding the rules of how AI is used in what we would call the kill chain — decisions of life and death. That should be legislated , because I don’t want someone to come in and change the rules, make them too onerous or too lax. So we took a stab at putting some left and right guardrails on the use of AI, and it’s really about keeping a human being as the ultimate decision maker. We all understand that AI is everywhere — even just doing a Google search, you’re using more AI on a regular basis, and so is the military. If it’s using AI to amalgamate health records or collect information about employees in a faster way, okay. That’s different from making final decisions on when and where to deploy a nuclear weapon, or domestic surveillance, or — again — decisions of life and death. AI is here. I don’t think AI is anywhere near ready to take over negotiations for anybody. And the other piece we got into the NDAA was the unbelievably rigorous testing we need before any of this stuff is fielded. That is the difference. In the race to get more and more technology into the Pentagon, we need to take a beat and make sure we’re properly testing — just like we would a new weapon system, just like we would a new part. I would say even more so, because of some of the advanced capabilities AI brings. You …
items.summaryZh: 密歇根州参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金在接受采访时讨论了国防授权法案（NDAA）标记日的议程，包括她推动的AI防护法案、禁止中国汽车进入美国军基地，以及对军方AI使用、投票安全和伊朗战争经费等关键条款的看法，说明这些立法对国家安全和监管透明度具有重要影响。
items.translationZh:
标题：参议员斯洛特金：NDAA、AI安全防护以及禁止中国汽车

正文：密歇根州参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金（曾在国防部作战局政策部门任职，并在众议院任职三届后加入参议院军备委员会），加入ChinaTalk，分拆哪些内容被写进法案、哪些被否决，以及为何NDAA标记日是参议院一年中为数不多的能像正常机构运作的两天。超出NDAA本身，我们还讨论……《AI防护法案》（AI Guardrails Act），Anthropic（安森公司）的争论，以及为何任何国防部长或AI公司都不应为“杀伤链”（kill chain）设定规则；她与伯尼·莫雷诺（Bernie Moreno）共同提出的禁止中国联网车辆的法案；如果民主党在十一月翻转议院的策略手册；数据所有权；中西部的数据中心反对声，以及为何一个健康的民主社会应当每天都在讨论AI。立即在您喜欢的播客应用收听。

NDAA标记——参议院最有趣的时光

Jordan Schneider：密歇根州参议员斯洛特金，曾在国防部作战局政策部门和美国众议院任职——欢迎来到ChinaTalk。

参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金：谢谢邀请。

Jordan Schneider：让我们先谈谈我们现在的NDAA草案。你对哪些内容感到兴奋或担忧？特别想聊一下ROBOCOM——机器人与自主系统作战指挥部，这是委员会期待启动的项目。

参议员斯洛特金：看，参议院在NDAA标记的那两天通常是我一年中最好的日子。它们在闭门会议中进行——这可能是人们行为更正常的原因——并且对条款进行真正的实质性辩论。最关键的是，如果一位共和党同僚提出一项条款，我会说，‘如果你把X、Y、Z改掉，我会支持你。’他们的工作人员回来修改X、Y、Z，三个小时后我们再审议，投票通过，条款进了法案。或者两位议员在不同党派有相似的修正案，他们可以在午餐时达成妥协并解决问题。这种情况在这里并不常见，所以我总是很高兴参与NDAA标记。

Jordan Schneider：在具体内容之前——是否有任何结构性改革可以让更多工作像NDAA标记日那样高效？

参议员斯洛特金：实现方式可能永远不会出现，因为这意味着把摄像机踢出去。这是唯一的区别——没有摄像机，人们就不觉得需要在公众面前表演。他们不再围绕政治论点说话，而是实际完成实质性工作。这是个很难的论点：‘美国，我们要在参议院降低透明度。’这很难。我认为有无数方法可以让参议院更高效。参议院曾是毛毛虫，未来可能变成蝴蝶，但现在仍在令人作呕的茧中，不知道将会怎样。我对如何改革这个机构有一百万个想法。但在NDAA标记时我们是有效的，因为人们不在为观众表演。

Jordan Schneider：人们真的需要这些摄像机吗？

参议员斯洛特金：我不确定人们早上醒来会说，‘我对今天在武装部队听证会上只能发言五分钟感到兴奋。’但这些听证会已经电视直播二十多年，没有人会成为那位说‘我要把公众踢出去’的主席。我也不认为公众希望对国会和参议院的工作缺少监督和洞察。因此这是个棘手的局面。解决办法是换一种我们使用这些委员会的愿景——我们如何工作，如何围绕大想法建立联盟。你刚才提到机器人指挥部，这是件大事——通过一项可能新建指挥部的法案——不幸的是我们要等到标记阶段才开始讨论。我们的工作人员在此之前已经在研究，但你可以想象有不同的做法。我对所有这些都持开放态度。但正如我所述，我喜欢NDAA标记，因为人们真的卷起袖子干实际工作。

Jordan Schneider：这里还有一个更广泛、奇怪的信息不对称问题。NDAA里内容繁多，作为委托人你只能了解其中少数条款。你如何管理这种委托‑代理问题？

参议员斯洛特金：和华盛顿的其他地方一样，你会有比你更聪明、沉浸在这些事务中的工作人员——他们阅读所有材料，梳理数百项修正案，标记最重要、最有争议、最具影响力、最‘性感’的部分。然后你准备。如果你做功课——我的一些同事真的很认真地做功课，有的则不然——如果你在意实质内容，你的工作人员会对你说，‘我们读完了两千页，这里有十九件我们认为你真的需要了解的、会真正进行实质性辩论的事项，并给出投票建议。’我们在我进入会议室前就会讨论所有这些。这里有装满修正案的巨型活页夹——这是一个完整的过程。但没有人能掌握每个细节。你得靠聪明的工作人员来恰当地标记出大的战略事项。

Jordan Schneider：很好。回到我的第一个问题——虽然我想花一小时讨论程序改革，也许下期节目再聊。

参议员斯洛特金：你需要生活。你需要生活。

Jordan Schneider：还有什么值得活着的？我昨晚看了尼克斯的比赛——太棒了。

参议员斯洛特金：我不喜欢尼克斯，但好吧。当你面对两千页文件时，确实有你极力争取并在意的内容，也有你不认同的内容。我真的很高兴进了的事项包括：AI防护法案——实际上为国防部使用AI（尤其是涉及生死的事务）设立了一些防护措施。禁止中国汽车进入所有美国军基地——这项工作我已经做了四年，适用于国内以及海外的基地，因为中国汽车随处可见。我们还有关于密歇根州萨尔福里奇空军基地（Selfridge）的重要条款，以及关于PFOS（全氟辛烷磺酸）污染的规定——确保军方在本地社区造成污染后负责清理，包括在密歇根州。随后有些失望的地方——包括我提出的禁止统一军人收集选票和投票机器、破坏选票链条的条款被否决。那本来是非法的，我们根本不该为此花钱。还有一项是禁止把统一军人派往投票站。我非常担心我们看到的一再出现的威权主义剧本，包括几个月前的匈牙利。总统曾说如果他的党在选举中未能胜出，那就是选举被操纵。我只是不想他们制造出国家安全威胁，以至于必须首次让军人去投票站——历史上从未这样做过。我们也真的没有讨论伊朗战争。这是屋子里一头巨大的大象。我们知道大家在谈论3500亿美元、4000亿美元的战争费用——而且战争还未结束。这场战争没有得到国会授权，那么我们会为一场未获授权的战争拨款吗？总额惊人，而他们却削减了国内事务的开支。因此在大局层面有重要的战略问题，也有很多让我欣慰的条款。

AI与杀伤链

Jordan Schneider：让我们继续这些主题。AI引发核战争的担忧是容易理解的共识。但当你从战略层面下沉到作战和战术层面时，会出现很多非常令人兴奋的应用。我刚在想——Claude Mythos（Anthropic的模型）能否取代你过去在国防部作战局的工作？我会更愿意让聊天机器人去谈判伊朗和平协议吗？也许！大概！什么是合理的，人们应当担心什么？因为显然如果你能正确使用这些工具，会在效率上有真实的优势。

参议员斯洛特金：在我们经济的各个领域，尤其在五角大楼，AI的实验早已展开。Anthropic（安森）争论提出的关键点是，不能让任何一位国防部长或任何一家公司决定AI在‘杀伤链’（即从情报到武器使用的全流程）中的规则——这涉及生死决策。应当由立法来规定，因为我不想让某人随意更改规则，使其过于严苛或宽松。所以我们尝试在AI使用上设定左右两端的防护措施，核心是让人类保持最终决策者的地位。我们都明白AI已无处不在——即使是一次Google搜索，你也在使用AI，军方也是如此。如果AI用于整合健康记录或更快收集员工信息，这还可以接受。但这与让AI在何时何地部署核武器、进行国内监控或其他生死决策完全不同。AI已在这里。我不认为AI已经准备好取代任何人的谈判工作。我们在NDAA中加入的另一项内容是对任何投入使用的AI系统进行极其严格的测试。这才是区别所在。在将更多技术推向五角大楼的竞争中，我们需要停下来，确保进行适当的测试——就像测试新武器系统或新零件一样。我甚至会说更需要，因为AI带来的高级能力。

（未完，受限于篇幅）
